page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.u1-c1m2.large is an x86_64 architecture virtual server belonging to the ecs.u1 server family, running on the KVM hypervisor. It features 2 dedicated physical cores with 2 vCPUs, powered by an Intel Xeon processor running at 2.5 GHz with a 33 MB L3 cache. The system is equipped with 4.0 GB of system memory, providing 2.0 GB of RAM per core. It does not include any local storage or GPU hardware, and features a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks indicate that the server delivers average single-core performance, with a stress-ng single-core CPU score of 1833.19 and a Geekbench single-core score of 1358.0. However, multi-core performance is limited, falling into the bottom 10% with a stress-ng multi-core score of 1918.58 and a Geekbench multi-core score of 1584.0. Memory bandwidth is also constrained, with read-only large block uncached speeds at 14.29 GB/sec and write speeds at 12.87 GB/sec.

From a resource density perspective, this instance offers a low-cost entry point for workloads that require dedicated CPU allocation but do not demand high memory density or local storage. The 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio makes it suitable for lightweight, compute-focused tasks rather than memory-intensive applications. Appropriate workloads include basic single-threaded applications, lightweight web serving, simple text processing, and small-scale development or testing environments.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.u1-c1m2.large is an x86_64 virtual server powered by a 2.5 GHz Intel Xeon processor with 2 dedicated vCPUs and 4.0 GB of RAM. Operating on the KVM hypervisor, this instance features a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio, 1 Gbps baseline network bandwidth, and no bundled local storage or GPU resources. Benchmark data shows average single-core performance, including a Geekbench single-core score of 1358.0, though multi-core and memory bandwidth metrics fall into the lower percentiles. This resource profile provides a cost-effective option for lightweight, single-threaded workloads, basic text processing, and small-scale development tasks that require dedicated CPU allocation without high memory demands.
